questions.text: Read the passage.Then answer the questions below. Many people like to eat pizza, but not everyone knows how to make it. Making the perfect pizza can be complicated, but there are lots of ways for you to make a more basic version at home. When you make pizza, you must begin with the crust. The crust can be hard to make. If you want to make the crust yourself, you will have to make dough using flour, water, and yeast. You will have to knead the dough with your hands. If you do not have enough time to do this, you can use a prepared crust that you buy from the store.After you have chosen your crust, you must then add the sauce. Making your own sauce from scratch can take a long time. You have to buy tomatoes, peel them, and then cook them with spices. If this sounds like too much work, you can also purchase jarred sauce from the store. Many jarred sauces taste almost as good as the kind you make at home.Now that you have your crust and your sauce, you need to add the cheese. Cheese comes from milk, which comes from cows. Do you have a cow in your backyard? Do you know how to milk the cow? Do you know how to turn that milk into cheese? If not, you might want to buy cheese from the grocery store instead of making it yourself.When you have the crust, sauce, and cheese ready, you can add other toppings. Some people like to put meat on their pizza, while others like to add vegetables. Some people even like to add pineapple! The best part of making a pizza at home is that you can customize it by adding your own favorite ingredients. As used in paragraph 4, which is the best definition for customize?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: In paragraph 4, customize means to make something personally. It shows that you can add your favorite ingredients to your pizza.

options.text: To prepare for more than one
options.explanation: This option suggests a plural approach, which does not align with the individual personalization described in the passage.
This option refers to a serving condition rather than customization, failing to capture the intended meaning of modifying something to suit personal taste.
This option talks about wanting something, which does not reflect the action of making changes to suit personal preferences.

questions.explanation: In paragraph 4, customize means to make something personally. It shows that you can add your favorite ingredients to your pizza.

questions.text: Read the passage. Then answer the questions below.Many people like to eat pizza, but not everyone knows how to make it. Making the perfect pizza can be complicated, but there are lots of ways for you to make a more basic version at home. When you make pizza, you must begin with the crust. The crust can be hard to make. If you want to make the crust yourself, you will have to make dough using flour, water, and yeast. You will have to knead the dough with your hands. If you do not have enough time to do this, you can use a prepared crust that you buy from the store.After you have chosen your crust, you must then add the sauce. Making your own sauce from scratch can take a long time. You have to buy tomatoes, peel them, and then cook them with spices. If this sounds like too much work, you can also purchase jarred sauce from the store. Many jarred sauces taste almost as good as the kind you make at home.Now that you have your crust and your sauce, you need to add the cheese. Cheese comes from milk, which comes from cows. Do you have a cow in your backyard? Do you know how to milk the cow? Do you know how to turn that milk into cheese? If not, you might want to buy cheese from the grocery store instead of making it yourself.When you have the crust, sauce, and cheese ready, you can add other toppings. Some people like to put meat on their pizza, while others like to add vegetables. Some people even like to add pineapple! The best part of making a pizza at home is that you can customize it by adding your own favorite ingredients.In paragraph 4, the author asks a series of questions in order to
questions.subject: English

options.text: Support the idea that most people cannot make homemade cheese
options.explanation: This option is correct because the author uses questions to illustrate the difficulties of making cheese at home, highlighting that many people lack the resources or knowledge to do so.

options.text: Reinforce the idea that most people probably live on farms
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the author's questions do not suggest that most people live on farms; rather, they emphasize the challenges of cheese-making for those who do not have access to cows.

options.text: Prove that store-bought cheese tastes better than homemade cheese
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as the passage does not compare the taste of store-bought cheese to homemade cheese; it focuses on the difficulty of making cheese rather than the quality of the final product.

options.text: Emphasize the superiority of homemade cheese over store-bought cheese
options.explanation: This option is also incorrect because the passage does not claim that homemade cheese is better; it simply discusses the challenges involved in making it, implying that many may prefer the convenience of buying cheese.

questions.explanation: In paragraph 4, the author asks questions like “Do you have a cow?” to show that making cheese at home is hard. These questions support the idea that most people cannot make homemade cheese and might need to buy it from the store.

questions.text: Read the passage. Then answer the questions below.Many people like to eat pizza, but not everyone knows how to make it. Making the perfect pizza can be complicated, but there are lots of ways for you to make a more basic version at home. When you make pizza, you must begin with the crust. The crust can be hard to make. If you want to make the crust yourself, you will have to make dough using flour, water, and yeast. You will have to knead the dough with your hands. If you do not have enough time to do this, you can use a prepared crust that you buy from the store.After you have chosen your crust, you must then add the sauce. Making your own sauce from scratch can take a long time. You have to buy tomatoes, peel them, and then cook them with spices. If this sounds like too much work, you can also purchase jarred sauce from the store. Many jarred sauces taste almost as good as the kind you make at home.Now that you have your crust and your sauce, you need to add the cheese. Cheese comes from milk, which comes from cows. Do you have a cow in your backyard? Do you know how to milk the cow? Do you know how to turn that milk into cheese? If not, you might want to buy cheese from the grocery store instead of making it yourself.When you have the crust, sauce, and cheese ready, you can add other toppings. Some people like to put meat on their pizza, while others like to add vegetables. Some people even like to add pineapple! The best part of making a pizza at home is that you can customize it by adding your own favorite ingredients.As used in paragraph 2, which is the best synonym for purchase?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option does not relate to acquiring something. 'Forget' means to not remember, which is the opposite of what is needed in this context.
'Buy' is the correct synonym for 'purchase', as both words involve obtaining something by paying for it. They are interchangeable in this context.
'Ask' means to request information or an action from someone. It does not convey the concept of acquiring something, making it an incorrect choice.
'Cook' refers to the process of preparing food. While it is related to making pizza, it does not serve as a synonym for 'purchase'.

questions.explanation: The best synonym for purchase in paragraph 3 is buy. It means to get something by paying for it

questions.text: Read the passage. Then answer the questions below. Mona doesn’t like to ask people for help. But it is hard for her to perform daily activities on her own. She is almost 13, yet she is no larger than a 5-year-old. Mona has trouble keeping her balance and can’t walk very far. When she uses a wheelchair, she can’t push it herself.Fortunately, Mona has a wonderful service dog named Sam. A service dog is a dog that has been trained to assist someone who has a physical problem. Sam lets Mona lean on him when she walks. He also pushes her wheelchair and turns the lights on and off. When Mona drops something, Sam picks it up. He even pulls her socks off at night.Sam also helps Mona with everyday tasks at school. He carries her books from class to class in a special backpack. He puts Mona’s completed assignments in her teachers’ homework trays. In the lunchroom, he throws away her trash. Besides making Mona less dependent on other people, Sam helps her lead a fuller life.Mona’s classmates flock around Sam like geese. This has helped her make friends. Sam also helps Mona be more active. With his aid, she raised over $500 in a walk-a-thon for her local humane society. Because of Sam, Mona doesn’t have to ask people for help. Sam brings her closer to the other kids. And he even helps her contribute to her community.In paragraph 4, the author writes, “Mona’s classmates flock around Sam like geese.” Which of the following literary techniques is used in this sentence?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Allusion, characterized by a reference to, or representation of, people, places, events, literary work, myths, or works of art, either directly or by implication.
options.explanation: Allusion involves referencing something outside the text, such as historical events or literary works. This sentence does not reference any external ideas or concepts, so this option is not correct.

options.text: Personification, characterized by giving human traits to nonhuman things, such as animals or objects.
options.explanation: Personification attributes human qualities to nonhuman subjects. The sentence does not give human traits to nonhuman entities but rather compares students to geese, making this option incorrect.

options.text: Irony, characterized by the use of words to express the opposite of their usual meaning.
options.explanation: Irony involves a contrast between expectation and reality. The sentence does not exhibit this contrast; it simply describes the behavior of the classmates, making this option incorrect.

options.text: Simile, characterized by the making of a comparison using the words “like” or “as.”
options.explanation: The sentence employs a simile by comparing the gathering of Mona's classmates around Sam to geese using 'like,' illustrating the similarity in their behavior. This makes this option the correct choice.

questions.explanation: The literary technique used in the sentence "Mona’s classmates flock around Sam like geese" is a simile. A simile is a figure of speech that directly compares two different things using the words "like" or "as" to highlight a similarity between them. In this case, the author is comparing Mona's classmates gathering around Sam to the way geese gather together, emphasizing the large and interested group of classmates surrounding the service dog.

questions.text: Read the passage. Then answer the questions below. When you imagine the desert, you probably think of a very hot place covered with sand. Although this is a good description for many deserts, Earth’s largest desert is actually a very cold place covered with ice: Antarctica.In order for an area to be considered a desert, it must receive very little rainfall. More specifically, it must receive an average of less than ten inches of precipitation—which can be rain, sleet, hail, or snow—on the ground every year. Antarctica, the coldest place on earth, has an average temperature that usually falls below the freezing point. And because cold air holds less moisture than warm air, the air in Antarctica does not hold much moisture at all. This is evident in the low precipitation statistics recorded for Antarctica. For example, the central part of Antarctica receives an average of less than 2 inches of snow every year. The coastline of Antarctica receives a little bit more, between seven and eight inches a year. Because Antarctica gets so little precipitation every year, it is considered a desert. When precipitation falls in hot deserts, it quickly evaporates back into the atmosphere. The air over Antarctica is too cold to hold water vapor, so there is very little evaporation. Due to this low rate of evaporation, most of the snow that falls to the ground remains there permanently, eventually building up into thick ice sheets. Any snow that does not freeze into ice sheets becomes caught up in the strong winds that constantly blow over Antarctica. These snow-filled winds can make it look as if it is snowing.Even though snowfall is very rare there, blizzards are actually very common in Antarctica. Based on the information in the final paragraph, it can be understood that blizzards in Antarctica are mainly the result of
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: While freezing temperatures are a characteristic of Antarctica, they do not directly cause blizzards, which are primarily influenced by wind patterns.
The passage mentions that snowfall is rare in Antarctica; therefore, large amounts of snowfall cannot be the reason for frequent blizzards.
Although low precipitation is a factor in defining a desert, it does not explain the occurrence of blizzards, which are more closely related to wind activity.
The passage explains that strong winds blow the little snow that falls around, which leads to the formation of blizzards, making this the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The passage explains that even though snowfall is rare, strong winds blow the snow around, which makes blizzards common in Antarctica.

questions.text: Read the passage. When you imagine the desert, you probably think of a very hot place covered with sand. Although this is a good description for many deserts, Earth’s largest desert is actually a very cold place covered with ice: Antarctica. In order for an area to be considered a desert, it must receive very little rainfall. More specifically, it must receive an average of less than ten inches of precipitation—which can be rain, sleet, hail, or snow—on the ground every year. Antarctica, the coldest place on earth, has an average temperature that usually falls below the freezing point. And because cold air holds less moisture than warm air, the air in Antarctica does not hold much moisture at all. This is evident in the low precipitation statistics recorded for Antarctica. For example, the central part of Antarctica receives an average of less than 2 inches of snow every year. The coastline of Antarctica receives a little bit more, between seven and eight inches a year. Because Antarctica gets so little precipitation every year, it is considered a desert. When precipitation falls in hot deserts, it quickly evaporates back into the atmosphere. The air over Antarctica is too cold to hold water vapor, so there is very little evaporation. Due to this low rate of evaporation, most of the snow that falls to the ground remains there permanently, eventually building up into thick ice sheets. Any snow that does not freeze into ice sheets becomes caught up in the strong winds that constantly blow over Antarctica. These snow-filled winds can make it look as if it is snowing. Even though snowfall is very rare there, blizzards are actually very common in Antarctica. Africa’s Sahara Desert is the second-largest desert on Earth. Based on the information in the passage, what characteristic must the Sahara share with Antarctica?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because, although Antarctica has low temperatures, the Sahara Desert is known for its high temperatures, making this characteristic not applicable to both.
This option is incorrect. The Sahara Desert is characterized by high temperatures, whereas Antarctica is known for its cold climate. Hence, this characteristic does not apply to both deserts.
This option is incorrect. While Antarctica may experience blizzards, the Sahara Desert does not experience blizzards at all, making this characteristic not shared between the two.
This is the correct option. Both Antarctica and the Sahara Desert are classified as deserts primarily due to their low precipitation levels, fulfilling the criteria for desert classification.

questions.explanation: The Sahara Desert, like Antarctica, is considered a desert because it gets very little rainfall. The passage explains that a desert must have low precipitation, which is the key characteristic that both deserts share.

questions.text: Read the passage. But in the main, I feel like a brown bag of miscellany propped against a wall. Against a wall in company with other bags, white, red, and yellow. Pour out the contents, and there is discovered a jumble of small things: priceless lengths of string, a key to a door long since crumbled away, a rusty knife-blade, old shoes saved for a road that never was and never will be a nail bent under the weight of things too heavy for any nail, a dried flower or two still a little fragrant. In your hand is the brown bag. On the ground before you is the jumble it held, so much like the jumble in the bags, could they be emptied, that all might be dumped in a single heap and the bags refilled without greatly altering the content of any. A bit of colored, glass more or less, would not matter. Perhaps that is how the Great Stuffer of Bags filled them in the first place-who knows? The primary purpose of the passage is to?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Describe the contents of the bags
options.explanation: This option misinterprets the passage's primary focus, which is not merely a description of the contents, but rather an exploration of their symbolic meaning.

options.text: Suggest people are not predisposed to prejudice
options.explanation: This option introduces an unrelated concept of prejudice, which is not addressed in the passage. The metaphor is more about value than social biases.

options.text: Recommend that all people share wealth with others
options.explanation: This option implies a call to action regarding wealth distribution, which is not the central theme of the passage. The focus is on the inherent value of individuals, not their material wealth.

options.text: Purport that all people have some value
options.explanation: This is the correct choice as the passage uses the metaphor of bags to illustrate that every individual possesses a unique collection of experiences and memories, signifying their inherent value.

questions.explanation: The passage uses the metaphor of bags to show that everyone has a mix of experiences and memories, suggesting that all people have inherent value. This is the primary purpose of the passage.

questions.text: Read the passage. It seemed to me, as I kept remembering all this, that those times and those summers had been infinitely precious and worth saving. There had been jollity and peace and goodness. The arrival at the beginning of August had been so big a business in itself; at the railway station the farm wagon drawn up, the first smell of the pine-laden air, the first glimpse of the smiling farmer, and the great importance of the trunks and your father's enormous authority in such matters, and the feel of the wagon under you for the long ten-mile haul, and at the top of the last long catching the first view of the lake after eleven months not seeing this cherished body of water. The shouts and cries of the other campers when they saw you, and the trunks to be unpacked, to give up their rich burden. (Arriving was less exciting nowadays, when you sneaked up in your car and parked it under a tree near the damp and took out the bags, and in five minutes it was all over, no fuss, no loud wonderful fuss about trunks.) The device used by the author in this passage is called:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Foreshadowing suggests hints or clues about future events, but this passage primarily reflects on past experiences rather than anticipating future ones.
Deja vu refers to the feeling of having experienced something before; however, this passage is not about a present experience but rather a reflection on the past.
Flashback accurately describes the author's recounting of past summers, interrupting the present narrative to provide a detailed memory, illustrating the nostalgia and significance of those moments.
Recollection involves remembering past events but does not specifically refer to the narrative technique of interrupting the flow of time, which is central to a flashback.

questions.explanation: Flashback accurately describes the author's recounting of past summers, interrupting the present narrative to provide a detailed memory.

questions.text: Read the passage. It seemed to me, as I kept remembering all this, that those times and those summers had been infinitely precious and worth saving. There had been jollity and peace and goodness. The arrival at the beginning of August had been so big a business in itself; at the railway station the farm wagon drawn up, the first smell of the pine-laden air, the first glimpse of the smiling farmer, and the great importance of the trunks and your father's enormous authority in such matters, and the feel of the wagon under you for the long ten- mile haul, and at the top of the last long catching the first view of the lake after eleven months not seeing this cherished body of water. The shouts and cries of the other campers when they saw you, and the trunks to be unpacked, to give up their rich burden. (Arriving was less exciting nowadays when you sneaked up in your car and parked it under a tree near the damp and took out the bags, and in five minutes it was all over, no fuss, no loud wonderful fuss about trunks.) In the passage, the author emphasizes which aspect of the childhood memory?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option suggests that the passage centers on the excitement leading up to the arrival. However, while anticipation is mentioned, it is not the primary focus of the author's reflections.
This option highlights the sensory elements, particularly the smell of the pine-laden air. While aromas contribute to the atmosphere, they are not the main theme the author emphasizes.

This option points to the social aspect of childhood memories. Although friends are part of the experience, the passage primarily emphasizes the arrival itself rather than the enjoyment of companionship.

This option correctly identifies the central theme of the passage. The author details the excitement and significance of the arrival at the lake, contrasting it with modern experiences to highlight its importance.

questions.explanation: The arrival is the central focus, with detailed descriptions of the journey, the excitement, and the contrast with present day arrivals.

questions.text: Read the passage. I fretted the other night at the hotel at the stranger who broke into my chamber after midnight, claiming to share it. But after his lamp had smoked the chamber full and I had turned round to the wall in despair, the man blew out his lamp, knelt down at his bedside, and made in a low whisper a long earnest prayer. Then was the relation entirely changed between us. I fretted no more,but respected and liked him. In line 1, "fretted" most nearly means:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option suggests a sense of curiosity, which does not align with the emotional turmoil expressed in the passage.
This option implies deep thought or reflection, but it fails to capture the feelings of distress described.
This option suggests a lack of clarity or strong feeling, which does not reflect the narrator's intense emotional reaction.
This choice accurately reflects the narrator's sense of anxiety and concern regarding the stranger in their chamber.

questions.explanation: "Worried" accurately captures the sense of anxiety and concern described in the passage.

questions.text: Read the passage. I think I knew General Washington intimately and thoroughly, and were I called on to delineate his character, it should be in terms like these. His mind was great and powerful, without being of the very first order; his penetration strong, though not so acute as that of a Newton, Bacon, or Locke; and as far as he saw, no judgment was ever sounder. It was slow in operation, being little aided by invention or imagination, but sure in conclusion. Hence, the common remark of his officers, of the advantage he derived from councils of war, where hearing all suggestions, he selected whatever was best; and certainly, no general ever planned his battles more judiciously. Why does the author write the first two lines (1-2) before writing about Washington?
questions.subject: English

options.text: As simply an introduction to establish his topic
options.explanation: This option suggests that the author is merely introducing Washington, which does not capture the nuance of the author's intent to establish credibility before discussing character traits.

options.text: To serve as a notification that he is about to expose Washington
options.explanation: This option implies a negative connotation, which is not supported by the author’s respectful tone and intent to present an informed analysis of Washington's character.

options.text: To prepare the reader for the limitations he found in Washington
options.explanation: While the author does discuss Washington's characteristics, this option inaccurately suggests that the author is primarily focused on Washington's limitations rather than providing a balanced view.

options.text: To qualify himself as one who is knowledgeable enough to fully present the subject
options.explanation: This option correctly identifies that the author is establishing his credibility and knowledge about Washington, which sets the stage for an informed discussion of his character.

questions.explanation: The first two lines establish the author’s knowledge and ability to present a detailed and nuanced assessment of Washington. This qualifies the author to discuss Washington’s character authoritatively.

questions.text: Read the passage. I think I knew General Washington intimately and thoroughly, and were I called on to delineate his character, it should be in terms like these. His mind was great and powerful, without being of the very first order; his penetration strong, though not so acute as that of a Newton, Bacon, or Locke; and as far as he saw, no judgment was ever sounder. It was slow in operation, being little aided by invention or imagination, but sure in conclusion. Hence the common remark of his officers, of the advantage he derived from councils of war, where hearing all suggestions, he selected whatever was best; and certainly, no general ever planned his battles more judiciously. According to the passage, which of the following best describes General Washington’s mind and character?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Extremely imaginative and quick in making decisions.
options.explanation: This option contradicts the passage, which states that Washington's thinking was slow and not imaginative.

options.text: Great and powerful, slow in operation but sure in conclusion.
options.explanation: This option accurately reflects the passage's description of Washington's mind as powerful, with a slow but certain decision-making process.

options.text: Weak in judgment but very inventive.
options.explanation: This option misrepresents Washington's judgment, which the passage describes as sound, and suggests he was inventive, which is not supported by the text.

options.text: As penetrating and acute as Newton, Bacon, or Locke.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect since the passage explicitly states that Washington's penetration was strong but not as acute as those figures.

questions.explanation: The passage describes Washington’s mind as powerful and great, though not the most brilliant like Newton or Locke. It emphasizes that his thinking was slow and not imaginative, but always led to sound and sure conclusions, making him a judicious general.

questions.text: Read the passage. To set down such choice experiences that my own writings may inspire me and, at last I may make wholes of parts. Certainly, it is a distinct profession to rescue from oblivion and to fix the sentiments and thoughts which visit all men more or less generally, that the contemplation of the unfinished picture may suggest its harmonious completion. Associate reverently and as much as you can with your loftiest thoughts. Each thought that is welcomed and recorded is a nest egg, by the side of which more will be laid. Thoughts accidentally thrown together become a frame in which more may be developed and exhibited. Perhaps this is the main value of a habit of writing, of keeping a journal, that so we remember our best hours and stimulate ourselves. My thoughts are my company. They have a certain individuality and separate existence, aye, personality. Having by chance recorded a few disconnected thoughts and then brought them into juxtaposition, they suggest a whole new field in which it was possible to labor and to think. Thought begat thought. The purpose of this passage is:
questions.subject: English

options.text: To provide a rationale for the thought process.
options.explanation: This option suggests that the passage is about explaining why we think, but it does not capture the emphasis on the act of writing as a means to cultivate thoughts.

options.text: To establish a reason for thinking, and the self-service thinking provides.
options.explanation: This option implies that the passage is primarily about the reasons for thinking, rather than highlighting the significance of writing as a transformative process.

options.text: To evidence that thinking is a reward unto itself.
options.explanation: This option indicates that the passage focuses on the intrinsic value of thinking, but it overlooks the central theme of writing as a tool for expression and development of thoughts.

options.text: To herald the writing of thoughts.
options.explanation: This option correctly identifies that the passage celebrates the act of writing as a means to capture, develop, and inspire thoughts, which aligns perfectly with the author's message.

questions.explanation: The passage highlights the importance of writing to capture and develop thoughts, aligning with the purpose of heralding the act of writing.

questions.text: Read the passage. When a luxury liner or a cargo ship nudges into her slip after an ocean crossing, her first physical contact with land is a heaving line. These streamers with a weight at the end, called a "monkey fist," arch gracefully from deck to pier. On board the ship, the heaving lines are tied to heavy, golden yellow Manila mooring lines. Longshoremen quickly pull in the heaving lines until they can fasten the mooring lines to iron bollards (posts). Soon, the ship is strung to her pier by four, eight, or as many as twenty-one 9-inch or 10-inch manila lines with perhaps a few wire ropes to stay motion fore and aft. The ship is secure against even the wrath of the storm or hurricane. A ship could dock without the aid of tugboats, and might have in New York in maritime strikes, but not without the lines to moor her to her berth. The maritime and the related fishing industry find perhaps 250 applications for rope and cordage. There are hundreds of different sizes, constructions, tensile strengths, and weights in rope and twine. Rope is sold by the pound but ordered by length and is measured by circumference rather than by diameter. The maritime variety is made chiefly from the fiber of the abaca, or manila plant, which is imported from the Philippines and Central America. Henequen from Mexico and Cuba, and sisal from Africa, the Netherlands East Indies, and other areas are also used, but chiefly for twine. Nylon is coming into increasing use, particularly by towing companies. But it is six times more expensive than Manila. However, nylon is much stronger, lighter in weight, and longer-wearing than manila. It is also more elastic and particularly adaptable for ocean towing. In docking a ship, the rope is:
questions.subject: English

options.text: Only a little less important than a tugboat
options.explanation: This option suggests that ropes are important but implies that tugboats are more crucial. The passage indicates that mooring lines are essential for docking, which contradicts this option.
The passage explicitly states that a ship could not dock without the lines to moor her to her berth, confirming that these lines are indeed absolutely essential for securing the ship.

This option downplays the importance of the lines, suggesting they are not required for docking. However, the passage makes it clear that these lines are fundamental for a ship's security while docked.

This option implies that mooring lines are infrequently utilized, which contradicts the passage's assertion about their critical role in docking ships. The passage highlights the widespread use of rope in the maritime industry.

questions.explanation: The passage clearly states that a ship could not dock without the lines to moor her to her berth, making rope absolutely essential for securing the ship.

questions.text: Read the passage. When a luxury liner or a cargo ship nudges into her slip after an ocean crossing, her first physical contact with land is a heaving line. These streamers with a weight at the end, called a "monkey fist," arch gracefully from deck to pier. On board the ship, the heaving lines are tied to heavy, golden yellow manila mooring lines. Longshoremen quickly pull in the heaving lines until they can fasten the mooring lines to iron bollards (posts). Soon the ship is strung to her pier by four, eight, or as many as twenty-one 9-inch or 10 inch manila lines with perhaps a few wire ropes to stay motion fore and aft. The ship is secure against even the wrath of the storm or hurricane. A ship could dock without the aid of tugboats, and might have in New York in maritime strikes, but not without the lines to moor her to her berth. The maritime and the related fishing industry find perhaps 250 applications for rope and cordage. There are hundreds of different sizes, constructions, tensile strengths, and weights in rope and twine. Rope is sold by the pound but ordered by length and is measured by circumference rather than by diameter. The maritime variety is made chiefly from the fiber of the abaca, or manila plant, which is imported from the Philippines and Central America. Henequen from Mexico and Cuba and sisal from Africa, the Netherlands East Indies, and other areas are also used, but chiefly for twine. Nylon is coming into increasing use, particularly by towing companies. But it is six times more expensive than manila. However, nylon is much stronger, lighter in weight, and longer-wearing than Manila. It is also more elastic and particularly adaptable for ocean towing. A monkey fist is a:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A monkey fist is not a device used for weaving; rather, it is a specific type of knot used as a weight at the end of a heaving line.
This option is incorrect. 'Monkey fist' is not a slang term; it specifically refers to a knot used in maritime contexts, not to people working in the industry.
This option is partially related but incorrect. A monkey fist is not a streamer itself; it is a weight that assists in throwing a heaving line, which may be used as a streamer.

options.text: Weight at the end of a rope
options.explanation: This is correct. A monkey fist is a weight, often made of a tightly knotted rope, attached to the end of a heaving line to assist in throwing it effectively, enabling secure mooring of ships.

questions.explanation: This is correct. A monkey fist is a weight, often made of a tightly knotted rope, attached to the end of a heaving line to help it be thrown more effectively.

questions.text: Read the passage. Use of electronic mail (e-mail) has been widespread for more than a decade. E-mail simplifies the flow of ideas, connects people from distant offices, eliminates the need for meetings, and often boosts productivity. However, e-mail should be carefully managed to avoid unclear and inappropriate communication. E-mail messages should be concise and limited to one topic. When complex issues need to be addressed, phone calls are still best. Which of the following would be the most appropriate title for the passage?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This title accurately reflects the passage's focus on how to effectively utilize e-mail while highlighting the need for careful management to avoid miscommunication.
This title suggests a focus on the widespread use of e-mail, but the passage primarily addresses its proper use and management, making this option less relevant.

options.text: E-Mail: The Ideal Form of Communication
options.explanation: This title implies that e-mail is the best communication method without acknowledging the potential drawbacks mentioned in the passage, such as the need for careful management.

options.text: Why Phone Calls Are Better Than E-Mail
options.explanation: This title contradicts the passage's overall message, which acknowledges the benefits of e-mail while noting that phone calls are preferable for complex issues, but does not declare them as superior.

questions.explanation: This is the most appropriate title. The passage discusses the advantages of e-mail, including its ability to simplify communication and boost productivity, while also emphasizing the need for careful management to avoid unclear or inappropriate messages.

questions.text: Read the passage. Patrick Henry is considered one of the great patriots of America's early history. He was a leader in every protest against British tyranny and in every movement for colonial rights, openly speaking against the unfair taxation and overly burdensome regulations imposed upon the American colonists by the British Parliament. In March 1775, Patrick Henry urged his fellow Virginians to arm themselves in self-defense. He spoke boldly in Richmond, Virginia, during the meeting of the state legislature. He closed that famous speech with the immortal words, "I know not what course others may take; but as for me, give me liberty or give me death." Which of the following would be the most appropriate title for this passage?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This title suggests a focus solely on Henry's speeches, rather than his overall impact as a patriot and leader during the revolutionary period.
This title accurately encapsulates the essence of the passage by emphasizing Henry's role as a patriot who fought against British tyranny and advocated for colonial rights.
This title is too broad and does not specifically highlight Patrick Henry's unique contributions and leadership as detailed in the passage.
This title incorrectly centers on the legislative body rather than on Patrick Henry's individual actions and speeches that were crucial to the American Revolution.

questions.explanation: This is the most appropriate title. The passage highlights Patrick Henry's role as a patriot, his leadership in resisting British tyranny, and his famous speech advocating for self defense.

questions.text: Read the passage. When winding an old clock, it is important not to overwind it. Overwinding occurs when the mainspring is almost fully wound, but the operator continues to turn the winding key. This causes the main spring to coil too tightly, and might even break it. This paragraph best supports the statement that:
questions.subject: English

options.text: Clocks have changed over the years.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the passage does not discuss the evolution or changes in clocks over time.

options.text: Old-fashioned clocks become fragile with age.
options.explanation: This option is misleading. While old clocks may be more delicate, the passage specifically addresses the mechanics of overwinding rather than age-related fragility.

options.text: Old-fashioned clocks were operated by an internal spring.
options.explanation: This option is correct. The passage refers directly to the mainspring, highlighting that old-fashioned clocks operate using an internal spring mechanism.

options.text: Overwinding clocks used to be a common mistake.
options.explanation: This option is plausible but too broad. The passage mentions overwinding without indicating its prevalence as a common mistake.

questions.explanation: This is supported by the paragraph. The discussion about the mainspring indicates that old-fashioned clocks operate with an internal spring mechanism.

questions.text: Read the passage. Critical reading is a demanding process. To read critically, you must slow down your reading and, with a pencil in hand, perform specific operations on the text. Mark up the text with your reactions, conclusions, and questions. When you read, become an active participant. This paragraph best supports the statement that:
questions.subject: English

options.text: Critical reading is a slow, dull, but essential process.
options.explanation: This option suggests that critical reading is uninteresting, which contradicts the idea of being an active participant in the reading process.

options.text: The best critical reading happens at critical times in a person's life.
options.explanation: This option implies that critical reading is situational, rather than a constant practice that requires engagement and attentiveness as described in the passage.

options.text: Readers should get in the habit of questioning the truth of what they read.
options.explanation: While questioning is part of critical reading, this option does not encompass the full scope of the active engagement and attentiveness emphasized in the passage.

options.text: Critical reading requires thoughtful and careful attention.
options.explanation: This option accurately reflects the essence of the passage, which highlights the necessity of slowing down, marking the text, and actively participating in the reading process.

questions.explanation: This is the most supported option. The paragraph describes critical reading as a demanding process that involves slowing down, marking up the text, and being an active participant, all of which require thoughtful and careful attention.

questions.text: Read the passage. To prepare for a career in engineering, a student must begin planning in high school. Mathematics and science should form the core curriculum. For example, in a school where sixteen credit hours are required for high school graduation, four should be in mathematics, one each in chemistry, biology, or physics. The remaining credits should include four in English and at least three in the humanities and social sciences.The average entering freshman in engineering should have achieved at least a 2.5 grade point average on a 4.0 scale in his or her high school. Although deficiencies can be corrected during the first year, the students who need additional work should expect to spend five instead of four years to complete a degree. What is the average grade point for an entering freshman in engineering?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A grade point average of 4.0 denotes a perfect score, which is not mentioned in the passage.
This is the correct answer. The passage clearly states that the average entering freshman in engineering should have at least a 2.5 grade point average on a 4.0 scale.
This option is incorrect. Although a 3.0 GPA is strong, the passage specifies that the minimum acceptable average for entering freshmen in engineering is 2.5.
This option is also incorrect. A 3.5 GPA is above the specified minimum, but the passage states that the average required is 2.5, making this option not relevant to the question.

questions.explanation: This is correct. The passage states that the average entering freshman in engineering should have at least a 2.5 grade point average on a 4.0 scale.

questions.text: Read the passage. Fear drains color from our faces, makes our teeth chatter, our hearts pound, our breath quickens, and our knees knock. Fear churns our stomachs, raises goosebumps, and causes jitters. Fear also raises feelings of anxiety and distrust, and may even cause us to change our behaviors. Obsessive fear can become a phobia that keeps us from enjoying life. What idea does the writer suggest?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Phobias keep us from enjoying life
options.explanation: This option directly reflects the passage's assertion that obsessive fear can lead to phobias, which hinder one’s ability to enjoy life.

options.text: Fear affects us in several ways
options.explanation: This option is partially correct; while the passage does mention various effects of fear, it does not capture the main idea that phobias specifically prevent enjoyment of life.

options.text: Fear is the result of distrust and anxiety
options.explanation: This option misinterprets the passage; the passage discusses how fear can lead to feelings of distrust and anxiety, not that fear itself is a result of these feelings.
This statement is too broad and does not encapsulate the specific point made in the passage about the detrimental effects of obsessive fear and phobias.

questions.explanation: The passage suggests that obsessive fear can become a phobia, which keeps us from enjoying life.

questions.text: Read the passage. There are two kinds of jewelry that I make. There is commercial jewelry class rings, necklaces, and the kinds of things most people wear. I sell these items to meet my expenses for raw materials, supplies, and to make a living. The other, more creative work I do makes me feel that I am developing as a craftsperson. The author of this passage implies that:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The passage does not mention the financial status of artists; rather, it discusses the different types of jewelry and their purposes.

options.text: There is no market for creative work
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The passage does not imply a lack of market for creative work; instead, it emphasizes the personal fulfillment that comes from creative endeavors.

options.text: Rings and necklaces cannot be creative
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The passage does not claim that commercial jewelry is devoid of creativity; it simply differentiates between commercial and creative purposes.

options.text: Commercial and creative work fulfill different needs for the artist
options.explanation: This option is correct. The passage implies that the artist engages in commercial work to meet practical financial needs while pursuing creative work for personal growth and satisfaction.

questions.explanation: This option is correct. The passage implies that commercial work is necessary for meeting practical needs like expenses, while creative work fulfills personal development and satisfaction for the artist.

questions.text: Read the passage. An increase in the level of serotonin in the human body is known to significantly enhance the mood of the person and, in some cases, help people overcome depression. Serotonin taken orally does not pass into the pathways of the central nervous system, because it does not cross the blood-brain barrier. However, tryptophan and its metabolite 5-hydroxytryptophan (5- HTP), from which serotonin is synthesized, do cross the blood-brain barrier. These agents are available as dietary supplements, and may be effective serotonergic agents. Which of the following can be correctly inferred from the statements above?
questions.subject: English

options.text: Individuals who do not consume enough tryptophan can develop depressive tendencies.
options.explanation: This option suggests a direct link between tryptophan intake and depression, which is not explicitly stated in the passage. While low levels of tryptophan may contribute to reduced serotonin production, the passage does not confirm that a lack of tryptophan directly leads to depression.

options.text: Individuals who consume tryptophan can metabolize enough serotonin and do not run the risk of depression.
options.explanation: This option overgeneralizes the effects of tryptophan. While tryptophan may help increase serotonin levels, the passage does not claim that consuming it guarantees immunity from depression; individual responses may vary.

options.text: Consumption of serotonin-laced foods can help cure depression in some cases.
options.explanation: This option misinterprets the information in the passage. It states that serotonin taken orally does not cross the blood-brain barrier, so consuming serotonin-laced foods would not effectively raise serotonin levels in the brain to address depression.

options.text: Tryptophan is an effective agent that can help cure depression in some cases.
options.explanation: This is correct. The passage highlights that tryptophan, along with 5-HTP, can cross the blood-brain barrier and are considered effective serotonergic agents, indicating their potential to enhance mood and alleviate depression.

questions.explanation: This is correct. The passage notes that tryptophan and its metabolite 5-HTP can cross the blood-brain barrier and are considered effective serotonergic agents, which suggests they may help with mood enhancement and potentially alleviate depression in some cases.

questions.text: Read the passage. The thunder did not break. Instead, the drops increased in number and suddenly became a flood. The trees, the hedges and, the fields, the sky itself, and all its gesticulating silent mobs wavered the reflections in a stream, and then dissolved into an air which was largely water.The word 'it' in line 02 refers to:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the passage does not suggest that 'it' refers to the sky. Instead, the sky is described as part of the environment affected by the flood, not the cause of 'it.'
This option is incorrect. While weather is related to the context of the passage, 'it' specifically refers to a more concrete outcome of the weather phenomena described, which is the flood.

This option is also incorrect. Although thunder is mentioned, the passage indicates that the thunder did not break, suggesting that 'it' refers to something that occurred as a result of the storm, rather than the sound itself.

This is the correct answer. The passage describes the transition from raindrops to an overwhelming flood. The phrase 'suddenly became a flood' directly indicates that 'it' refers to the flood that resulted from the increasing rainfall.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. The passage describes how the rain increased and eventually resulted in a flood. The phrase "the drops increased in number and suddenly became a flood" implies that "it" refers to this flood, which is the culmination of the increasing rainfall.

questions.text: Read the passage. Reality TV shows will have an adverse effect on traditional dramas and comedies. As Reality TV increases in popularity, network executives will begin canceling more traditional programs and replacing them with the latest in Reality TV. All the given statements are true regarding the adverse effects of Reality TV except:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This statement is true as the passage indicates that network executives will cancel traditional programs in favor of Reality TV.

options.text: Broadcasting of the latest programs of current interest
options.explanation: This statement aligns with the passage's assertion that Reality TV's rise in popularity will lead to the broadcasting of more current interest programs, making it true.
This statement is true, as the passage notes the increasing popularity of Reality TV as a reason for the adverse effects on traditional programming.

options.text: Traditional dramas have tame scripts and stories
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect as it is not mentioned in the passage. The focus is on the impact of Reality TV, not on the nature of traditional dramas' scripts or stories.

questions.explanation: This statement is not mentioned in the passage. The passage focuses on the impact of Reality TV on traditional programs but does not provide details about the nature of traditional dramas' scripts or stories.

questions.text: Read the passage. When writing business letters or memos, it is not practical to be personal. Though the first person point of view may make the reader feel close to the writer, it also implies a certain subjectivity. That is, the writer is expressing a personal view from a personal perspective. All are impractical approaches towards writing business letters and memos, except:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This statement is correct. Being impersonal helps to maintain objectivity and professionalism, which are essential in business communication.
This approach aligns with the idea of being impersonal, but it is less specific than simply stating 'to be impersonal' and does not directly address the question's context.
This option is incorrect because using the first-person point of view introduces subjectivity and is typically impractical in business writing.
This option is incorrect as it suggests that all provided approaches are impractical, while 'to be impersonal' is actually a valid and practical approach.

questions.explanation: This statement is correct. Being impersonal is a practical approach in business writing because it maintains objectivity and professionalism.

questions.text: Read the passage. What if you vote for me? I assure you that your taxes will be very low, the government will provide free education, and there will be equality and justice for all citizens. Cast your vote for me today. The tone of the sentence is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This tone typically provides details and descriptions rather than attempting to persuade or convince. It does not align with the intent of the passage.
This tone seeks to convince or persuade the audience to take a certain action or adopt a particular viewpoint, which aligns with the campaign promises made in the statement.
This tone tells a story or recounts events. The passage is not focused on storytelling but rather on influencing the audience's decision.
This tone is used to explain or inform. The passage is not merely providing information but is actively trying to persuade the reader.

questions.explanation: This tone seeks to convince or persuade the audience to take a certain action or adopt a particular viewpoint, which aligns with the campaign promises made in the statement.

questions.text: Read the passage. If only there were some decent jobs out there, I wouldn't be reduced to living in this miserable dump. The tone of the sentence is:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This tone conveys a sense of resentment or dissatisfaction, reflecting the speaker's frustration about their circumstances and the lack of good job opportunities.
This tone would suggest a contrast between expectation and reality, but the passage lacks that nuance and instead expresses straightforward dissatisfaction.
This tone implies a light-hearted or comedic attitude, which is not present in the passage as it discusses serious issues of living conditions.
This tone indicates a hopeful outlook, which contradicts the expression of despair and frustration found in the passage.

questions.explanation: This tone conveys a sense of resentment or dissatisfaction, which fits with the expression of frustration about the lack of decent jobs and the resulting poor living conditions.

questions.text: Read the passage. Yet though we look alike, we have very different dispositions. You could say that we're opposites. While I'm often quiet and pensive, Andy is loud and doesn't seem to stop to think about anything. We can therefore conclude that pensive means:
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option suggests a quality of having knowledge or understanding, but it does not capture the reflective nature implied in the passage.
This is the correct answer, as 'pensive' directly refers to someone who is engaged in serious thought, perfectly matching the description of being quiet and thoughtful.
While being considerate may involve thoughtfulness, it does not specifically relate to the internal contemplation suggested by 'pensive'.
This option describes someone who tends to argue or be combative, which contrasts sharply with the reflective and quiet nature described in the passage.

questions.explanation: "Pensive" describes someone who is often absorbed in deep thought or reflection. This aligns with the description of being quiet and thoughtful in the passage.

questions.text: Read the passage. The lovely egret is in danger of extinction because clothing manufacturers use their long, beautiful tail feathers to make ladies' hats. What is an egret?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An egret is indeed a type of bird, recognized for its striking long feathers. The context provided in the passage, concerning the use of its feathers in fashion, specifically relates to avian characteristics.
This option is incorrect because the passage discusses an animal, specifically a bird, rather than a human or child. There is no indication in the passage that relates to children.
This option is incorrect as it suggests a type of big cat, which is unrelated to the content of the passage discussing a bird. The passage makes no mention of tigers or any feline species.
This option is incorrect because the passage is focused on a specific type of bird and its plight, rather than any aspect of human society or civilization.

questions.explanation: An egret is a type of bird known for its long, elegant plumage. The passage specifically refers to the egret's feathers being used in fashion, which indicates it is a bird species.
